overlays.blurb: 2.7% ad-valorem on robot spending (their 1-3.7% sufficient statistic, central).
overlays.name: Robot tax — optimal (Costinot-Werning)

overlays.blurb: 5.1% ad-valorem, their decade-1 Mirrleesian rate (steady state: zero).
overlays.name: Robot tax — transitional (GRT)

overlays.blurb: A worker-based UBI financed with a pure tax clawback.
overlays.name: UBI $12k + 30% recapture

overlays.blurb: Tax the compute-capital pool like domestic capital (ETR 0.27) instead of 0.05.
overlays.name: Compute-pool parity taxation

overlays.blurb: Government equity share of after-tax automation profit; revenue scales with the shock.
overlays.name: Sovereign wealth fund — 20% of AI profits

overlays.blurb: A new broad consumption tax — their primary post-labor instrument; base erodes as displacement deepens.
overlays.name: Federal VAT 5% (Korinek-Lockwood stage 1)

presets.blurb: His 10-year upper bounds at face value: small exposed share, modest productivity, normal labor market, no wage response.
presets.name: Acemoglu — Modest AI

adoption.text: 23% of exposed tasks profitably automatable within 10y (Svanberg et al. extrapolation) → end 0.23; start 0.02 per Canaries realized pace (§1)

cognitive_feasibility.text: Acemoglu 2024: 19.9% of the US wage bill exposed to AI (§1)

demand_multiplier.text: active-Fed reading; Chodorow-Reich 1.8 is the no-offset pole (§1)

physical_feasibility.text: his frame is cognitive-only; token robot share (§1)

retained_profit_share.text: capital share +0.38pp, 'no sizable wage rises' → survivor 0.05 (§1)

presets.blurb: AI complements more than it substitutes: slow realized adoption, gains shared with survivors, strong productivity, mild scarring.
presets.name: Brynjolfsson — Augmentation

adoption.text: Canaries: cumulative ~0.01-0.03 at year 3 → start 0.02; moderate end (§1)

cognitive_feasibility.text: Eloundou β central ~0.30 of task content (§1)

productivity_passthrough.text: GenAI at Work +15% avg / +30% novices → 0.5 for the cognitive channel (§1)

survivor_elasticity.text: complementarity at low depth: novices +30% (GenAI at Work) → +0.10 (§1)

presets.blurb: Their Medium scenario translated: 60% exposed, half automated in 10y, heavy scarring, high value capture, capital taxed at their ETR. Direct comparator (10y target -2.8%).
presets.name: Windfall Trust — Medium

adoption.text: their Medium: 50% of exposed displaced over the decade (§2③)

cognitive_feasibility.text: Windfall: exposed sectors = 60% of jobs; 0.55 cog + 0.20 phys ≈ their combined share (§1)

reemployment_haircut.text: their Medium scarring: re-employment at 70% of prior wage (§2③)

retained_profit_share.text: their high-capture: firms 45% / consumers 45% of displaced wages → 0.50/0.50 of net saving (§2③)

presets.blurb: A moderate shock met by the labor market ADH actually measured: decade-scale adjustment, LFP exit dominant, deep scarring, full demand amplification. 15 years.
presets.name: China-Shock Grind

cognitive_feasibility.text: moderate exposure; the mechanism, not the size, is the story (§2④)

lfp_exit_rate.text: ADH: NILF +0.55pp vs unemployment +0.22pp — LFP exit dominates → 0.08-0.15 (§1)

physical_feasibility.text: includes the manufacturing channel ADH measured (§2④)

productivity_passthrough.text: below shipped: gains real but local demand collapses (§2④)

reabsorption_rate.text: ADH: employment-to-population falls ~1:1 for a decade → 0.05-0.10/yr (§1)

retained_profit_share.text: capital keeps most of the gains (§2④)

survivor_elasticity.text: Webb annualized wage drag, deep-slack end (§1)

presets.blurb: Full automation over 20 years; wages collapse before the end; capital keeps the gains; states lean on spending cuts.
presets.name: Korinek-Suh — AGI in 20 years

adoption.text: linear to full automation over the 20y horizon (§2⑤)

baseline_growth_rate.text: upper band that keeps %-GDP denominators interpretable (§1)

cognitive_feasibility.text: Korinek-Suh baseline AGI: all tasks automatable within 20y (§1)

compute_effective_rate.text: GRT post-TCJA effective rate: capital undertaxed (§1)

physical_feasibility.text: AGI includes physical work, behind the capacity ramp (§2⑤)

productivity_passthrough.text: near lever ceiling in AGI scenarios (§1)

reabsorption_rate.text: no recovery in their AGI scenarios: wages stay collapsed (§1)

retained_profit_share.text: capital share → 1 as labor share collapses (Korinek-Lockwood) (§1)

robotics_lag.text: physical capacity trails cognition by ~a decade (§2⑤)

state_cut_share.text: labor-base rate hikes cannot close AGI-stage gaps (Korinek-Lockwood) (§1)

survivor_elasticity.text: wage collapse ~3y before full automation → slider max -0.50 (§1)

presets.blurb: The aggressive transition: full automation at year 5 (kinked path), viewed over a 10-year fiscal window. The stress case.
presets.name: Korinek-Suh — AGI in 5 years

adoption.text: kinked: linear to 1.0 at year 5, flat after — their wage collapse hits ~year 3 (§1)

cognitive_feasibility.text: Korinek-Suh aggressive AGI: 5 years to full automation (§1)

presets.blurb: The AI Futures scenario shape: cognition maxes almost immediately, robots ramp in 3 years, heavy compute investment, 8-year horizon.
presets.name: AI 2027 — Fast takeoff

cognitive_feasibility.text: superhuman coder → ASI within ~1y of scenario start (§1)

reemployment_haircut.text: displaced land at the service floor (§2⑦)

presets.blurb: AI Futures' managed transition: US-China consortium slows the takeoff; 95% of tasks automatable by 2035-36; employment 62%→12% over 2032-2040. World state only — their permit-fee/Citizen's-Dividend regime is policy, not modeled here. 14 years (2027-2040).
presets.name: AI 2040 — Plan A (The Deal)

adoption.text: kinked: reach 1.0 at year 9 (2036: 'pretty much everything'); value-weighted AI/robot labor share 20% (2032) → 85% (2035). Our linear kink front-loads the mid-path drop vs their flat-to-2032 employment — their early stability comes from new-job creation the model maps onto reabsorption (§2⑧)

auto_cost.text: 'corporations reinvesting nearly all their revenue into datacenters, factories, robots' → slider-max capex share 0.30 (§2⑧)

cognitive_feasibility.text: AI 2040 App.X n1: AI automates 50% of cognitive tasks by 2032, 95% by 2035 (would be 100% but for bans) → terminal 0.95 (§2⑧)

compute_effective_rate.text: status quo 0.05; their permit fees (10× federal revenue by 2032) are the policy question, not the world state (§2⑧)

demand_multiplier.text: managed transition; in-world dividends cushion demand but are policy — moderate 1.0 under current law (§2⑧)

interest_rate.text: their ~100% real rates unrepresentable; Korinek-Lockwood ~4% for cross-preset comparability (§2⑧)

lfp_exit_rate.text: orderly exit; their voluntary dividend-living maps to NILF under current law (employment rate 62%→12%) (§2⑧)

physical_feasibility.text: robots 35% of physical tasks 2032, 95% by 2036 (App.X n1) (§2⑧)

price_reduction_share.text: 'goods and services generally falling in price' (App.X #4) (§2⑧)

productivity_passthrough.text: GDP growth ~100%/yr 2032-2037 — far beyond the lever ceiling; 0.90 = near max, magnitude unrepresentable (documented) (§2⑧)

reabsorption_rate.text: 2032-34: displaced white-collar 'get new jobs doing things AIs can't do' — positions explicitly temporary; low-moderate 0.15 (§2⑧)

retained_profit_share.text: firms reinvest nearly all revenue (capex expensing zeroes taxable income — their fn42); 0.55/0.30/0.15 split (§2⑧)

robotics_lag.text: robot channel full at 2036 = year 9 from the 2027 start (§2⑧)

survivor_elasticity.text: human-bottlenecked work rises in price; remaining 5% of tasks pay ~$120K/worker (fn48) → mild complementarity +0.10 (§2⑧)

presets.blurb: AI Futures' no-deal branch: full AI R&D automation 2030, superintelligence early 2031, integration 'as fast as markets allow' — their AI 2027 Race ending on a 2030 fuse. 10 years (2027-2036).
presets.name: AI 2040 — Plan D (The Race)

adoption.text: kinked: reach 1.0 at year 7 (2034) — takeoff 2030-31 then 'integrated into everything approximately as fast as the markets and laws allow'; their no-regulation counterfactual is >1000× growth by 2033 (App.X) (§2⑨)

cognitive_feasibility.text: 'ASI early 2031, 10,000× AI R&D speedup' — no pause, no bans; full cognitive feasibility (§2⑨)

compute_effective_rate.text: light-touch regulation: capital undertaxed status quo (§2⑨)

demand_multiplier.text: no-offset crisis regime (WW3-risk world, no dividend) (§2⑨)

lfp_exit_rate.text: mass exit in the crash regime (§2⑨)

reabsorption_rate.text: no managed transition, no dividend, no time — AI-2027-race value (§2⑨)

retained_profit_share.text: no deal, no permits, no sharing: capital keeps it; 0.80/0.15/0.05 (§2⑨)

presets.blurb: The NBER expert survey's rapid scenario at its elicited 14% probability: strong growth, modest displacement that exits the labor force rather than the unemployment rolls, and a falling labor share.
presets.name: Karger et al. — Expert survey, rapid (P≈14%)

adoption.text: calibrated: net employment decline ≈3.1% by 2030 — the survey's rapid AI-attributable LFP exit ≈1.7pp plus displacement churn (w35046, §5)

cognitive_feasibility.text: rapid = AI surpasses most cognitive tasks; adoption, not capability, binds (usage ≈1/3 of feasibility, Anthropic §8)

demand_multiplier.text: no demand doom loop in the expert median (§5)

lfp_exit_rate.text: the survey's signature: unemployment stays 5-6%; displacement exits the labor force (~10M by 2050) → elevated 0.06 (§5)

physical_feasibility.text: rapid includes physical tasks late; token share at 2030 (§5)

productivity_passthrough.text: 0.95, near the model ceiling; the model still books only +3.0% GDP by 2030 vs the survey's +7.2% — AI-broad TFP gains beyond automated tasks sit outside the no-offsets frame (documented, §5)

reab_wage_baumol.text: service wages ride growth; median income rises under rapid (§5)

reabsorption_rate.text: 0.35 mid-slack, churn consistent with stable unemployment (§5)

robotics_lag.text: physical capability arrives late in the window (§5)

survivor_elasticity.text: raises arrive funded via the survivor share, not tightness (§5)

presets.blurb: The Labor Automation Hub's community medians: employment ≈7.5% below the no-AI baseline by 2035, labor share down ~4pp, survivor wages up — displacement without a doom loop.
presets.name: Metaculus — Crowd median, 2035

adoption.text: calibrated: net employment −7.3% by 2035 vs the crowd's −4.44% plus the +3.1% no-AI baseline ⇒ ≈7.5% AI-attributed gap (labor hub, §6)

demand_multiplier.text: commentary flags a consumption problem; mid value (§6)

lfp_exit_rate.text: long-term unemployment stays ≤4.5%: exits, not unemployment (§6)

physical_feasibility.text: forecasters expect robotics in warehouse work by 2035 (§6)

productivity_passthrough.text: crowd labor productivity +28.6% by 2035 is economy-wide; the model books the automation-linked part (§6)

reab_wage_baumol.text: service wages rise with tight refuge demand (§6)

reab_wage_crowding.text: crowding pressure from the displaced inflow (§6)

survivor_elasticity.text: crowd real median wage +4.9% is partly composition; the like-for-like W here reaches +2.3% via the funded share (§6)

presets.blurb: Their four archetypes read as a decade of displacement pressure: the 18% high-automation-risk cohort plus partial staffing compression where jobs reorganize — explicitly a map of pressure, not a job-loss forecast.
presets.name: OpenAI — Jobs transition framework

adoption.text: 18% high-risk + ~a third of the 24% reorganize cohort's staffing compression ⇒ ceiling ≈0.20 of feasible work, reached ~2033 (§7)

lfp_exit_rate.text: near-normal exit; pressure ≠ separation in their frame (§7)

physical_feasibility.text: 41% of employment is physically gated; barely touched (§7)

price_reduction_share.text: elastic-demand occupations expand as costs fall (§7)

robotics_lag.text: LLM-era frame; robotics outside the window (§7)
